current_turn_information: I am a 70-year-old F patient. I haven't been feeling well recently. My main issue is: Assess and diagnose the 70-year-old female patient based on the available clinical presentation.

query.query_text: 70-year-old female presenting with nonspecific systemic symptoms; need targeted history and exam before final diagnosis; avoid premature closure

positive_experience.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
positive_experience.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

positive_experience.situation: When hip pain is described as aching and localized with stiffness and functional limitation, but no imaging or labs are available, what should the doctor avoid?
positive_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without imaging or physical exam findings, as this may lead to premature closure and miss alternative diagnoses like infection or fracture.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when imaging or physical exam is missing; do not use if imaging is available and shows clear osteoarthritis or if the patient has systemic symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.

positive_experience.situation: When evaluating a 54-year-old asymptomatic female with no reported symptoms, the initial uncertainty is whether routine screening for colorectal cancer is indicated despite the absence of red flags.
positive_experience.action: Request a targeted history for gastrointestinal symptoms, weight loss, or systemic signs, then confirm absence of prior GI conditions or relevant lab results before finalizing screening recommendation.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when patient is asymptomatic and age is 50+; do not use if patient reports symptoms, has known GI disease, or has abnormal labs or imaging findings.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with redness, swelling, and pus around a nail, and the history suggests recent trauma or nail-biting, but no imaging or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Update the differential to include paronychia and confirm the diagnosis with targeted patient history about trauma or nail manipulation, then finalize only after ruling out onychia or other nail infections.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms suggest paronychia and no imaging or labs are available; do not use if there is evidence of nail bed involvement or if the patient has systemic signs of infection.

negative_experience.situation: When a 63-year-old woman presents with non-specific symptoms and denies any systemic signs, and initial targeted history and labs are unhelpful, the doctor should avoid broad differential updates without focusing on visual or vascular clues.
negative_experience.action: Request a focused ophthalmologic exam or review of any available eye imaging to rule out retinal vascular events, especially if the patient is elderly and has no clear systemic explanation.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient is elderly with non-specific complaints and no systemic signs; do not use if there is a clear history of systemic illness, or if eye exam/imaging is unavailable or not indicated.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with non-specific symptoms and no clear diagnostic direction, and initial targeted questions yield unhelpful or negative responses, the doctor should avoid premature finalization and instead broaden the differential to include psychiatric or systemic conditions.
negative_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without ruling out psychiatric conditions; instead, broaden the differential to include schizophrenia and other psychiatric diagnoses, especially when non-specific symptoms persist despite negative history and labs.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms are non-specific and history is unhelpful; do not use if clear physical exam or imaging findings point to a structural or infectious cause.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with non-specific symptoms and no clear diagnosis, and initial targeted history and lab/imaging requests yield no findings, the doctor should avoid premature finalization without ruling out coagulation disorders.
negative_experience.action: Delay final diagnosis until coagulation labs or imaging are reviewed, or until a targeted question about bleeding or bruising is asked.
negative_experience.boundary: Use when patient has non-specific symptoms and no clear diagnosis, but only if coagulation labs or bleeding history are not yet reviewed; do not use if bleeding or bruising history is already known or if labs/imaging are unavailable.

arguments.question: Have you experienced any recent weight loss, fatigue, fever, night sweats, or any other systemic symptoms?

clinical_turn.patient_or_tool_response: {'question': 'I’ve been feeling tired and weak lately. I also noticed my skin has a yellowish tint, and my liver and spleen seem to be enlarged. I’ve been getting more shortness of breath and sometimes feel dizzy. I haven’t had any fever or night sweats, but I do have some heart palpitations. My skin has also taken on a bronze color recently. I’ve been experiencing some headaches too.'}

# Signs and Symptoms

[Document 2] (ID: 14689_2, Score: 0.865)
# Screening
Routine\xa0screening\xa0of the general population for hereditary hemochromatosis, that is, by genetic testing, has been evaluated by the US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF), among other groups. In case-finding for hereditary hemochromatosis, serum ferritin and transferrin saturation tes...

[Document 3] (ID: 3471_10, Score: 0.865)
When patients present with symptoms, hemochromatosis should be considered when there are complaints of fatigue, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, arthralgias, (typically of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints), chondrocalcinosis, impotence, decreased libido, and symptoms of heart fail...

[Document 4] (ID: 27372_1, Score: 0.858)
# Neonatal and perinatal hemochromatosis
A rare fulminant disease of the liver, of unknown cause, characterized by massive...[truncated]'}}
